title: Kristallnacht

infobox.value: [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") (then including [Austria](/wiki/Austria_within_Nazi_Germany "Austria within Nazi Germany") and the [Sudetenland](/wiki/Sudetenland "Sudetenland"))

[Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler"), *[Sturmabteilung](/wiki/Sturmabteilung "Sturmabteilung")* (SA) stormtroopers, *[Schutzstaffel](/wiki/Schutzstaffel "Schutzstaffel")* (SS), [Hitler Youth](/wiki/Hitler_Youth "Hitler Youth"), German civilians
Revenge for [Ernst vom Rath's assassination](/wiki/Assassination_of_Ernst_vom_Rath "Assassination of Ernst vom Rath"), [antisemitism](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism")

| * [History](/wiki/History_of_antisemitism "History of antisemitism") * [Timeline](/wiki/Timeline_of_antisemitism "Timeline of antisemitism") * [Reference](/wiki/List_of_writers_on_antisemitism "List of writers on antisemitism")

([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Novemberpogrome*, pronounced [[noˈvɛm.bɐ.poˌɡʁoːmə]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Novemberpogrome.ogg "File:De-Novemberpogrome.ogg")),[[1]](#cite_note-Britannica-1)[[2]](#cite_note-nhuk-2)[[3]](#cite_note-toi-3) was a [pogrom](/wiki/Pogrom "Pogrom") against [Jews](/wiki/Jew "Jew") carried out by the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party")'s [*Sturmabteilung* (SA)](/wiki/Sturmabteilung "Sturmabteilung") and [*Schutzstaffel* (SS)](/wiki/Schutzstaffel "Schutzstaffel") paramilitary forces along with some participation from the [Hitler Youth](/wiki/Hitler_Youth "Hitler Youth") and German civilians throughout [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") on 9–10 November 1938. The German authorities looked on without intervening.[[4]](#cite_note-Gilbert42-4) The euphemistic name *Kristallnacht* comes from the shards of broken glass that littered the streets after the windows of Jewish-owned stores, buildings, and [synagogues](/wiki/Synagogue "Synagogue") were smashed. The pretext for the attacks was the assassination, on 9 November 1938, of the German diplomat [Ernst vom Rath](/wiki/Ernst_vom_Rath "Ernst vom Rath")[[5]](#cite_note-book-on-grynszpan-5) by [Herschel Grynszpan](/wiki/Herschel_Grynszpan "Herschel Grynszpan"), a 17-year-old German-born [Polish Jew](/wiki/Polish_Jew "Polish Jew") living in Paris.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

1938 anti-Jewish pogroms in Nazi Germany

For the John Zorn album, see [*Kristallnacht* (album)](/wiki/Kristallnacht_(album) "Kristallnacht (album)").

"Crystal Night" redirects here. For the albums, see [Attacco Decente](/wiki/Attacco_Decente "Attacco Decente") and [Carlos Toshiki & Omega Tribe](/wiki/Carlos_Toshiki_%26_Omega_Tribe "Carlos Toshiki & Omega Tribe").

***Kristallnacht*** (German pronunciation: [[kʁɪsˈtalnaχt]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Kristallnacht.ogg "File:De-Kristallnacht.ogg") lit. 'crystal night') or the **Night of Broken Glass**, also called the **November pogrom(s)** ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Novemberpogrome*, pronounced [[noˈvɛm.bɐ.poˌɡʁoːmə]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Novemberpogrome.ogg "File:De-Novemberpogrome.ogg")),[[1]](#cite_note-Britannica-1)[[2]](#cite_note-nhuk-2)[[3]](#cite_note-toi-3) was a [pogrom](/wiki/Pogrom "Pogrom") against [Jews](/wiki/Jew "Jew") carried out by the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party")'s [*Sturmabteilung* (SA)](/wiki/Sturmabteilung "Sturmabteilung") and [*Schutzstaffel* (SS)](/wiki/Schutzstaffel "Schutzstaffel") paramilitary forces along with some participation from the [Hitler Youth](/wiki/Hitler_Youth "Hitler Youth") and German civilians throughout [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") on 9–10 November 1938. The German authorities looked on without intervening.[[4]](#cite_note-Gilbert42-4) The euphemistic name *Kristallnacht* comes from the shards of broken glass that littered the streets after the windows of Jewish-owned stores, buildings, and [synagogues](/wiki/Synagogue "Synagogue") were smashed. The pretext for the attacks was the assassination, on 9 November 1938, of the German diplomat [Ernst vom Rath](/wiki/Ernst_vom_Rath "Ernst vom Rath")[[5]](#cite_note-book-on-grynszpan-5) by [Herschel Grynszpan](/wiki/Herschel_Grynszpan "Herschel Grynszpan"), a 17-year-old German-born [Polish Jew](/wiki/Polish_Jew "Polish Jew") living in Paris.

Jewish homes, hospitals and schools were ransacked as attackers demolished buildings with sledgehammers.[[6]](#cite_note-Gilbert13-6) Rioters destroyed over 1,400 synagogues and prayer rooms throughout Germany, Austria, and the [Sudetenland](/wiki/Sudetenland "Sudetenland").[[7]](#cite_note-Korn-7)[[8]](#cite_note-Evans-8) Over 7,000 Jewish businesses were damaged or destroyed,[[9]](#cite_note-Berenbaum2005p49-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Gilbert30-10) and [30,000 Jewish men were arrested](/wiki/Mass_arrests_after_Kristallnacht "Mass arrests after Kristallnacht") and incarcerated in [concentration camps](/wiki/Nazi_concentration_camps "Nazi concentration camps").[[11]](#cite_note-Atlantic-11) British historian [Martin Gilbert](/wiki/Martin_Gilbert "Martin Gilbert") wrote that no event in the history of German Jews between 1933 and 1945 was so widely reported as it was happening, and the accounts from foreign journalists working in Germany drew worldwide attention.[[6]](#cite_note-Gilbert13-6) *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* of London observed on 11 November 1938: "No foreign propagandist bent upon blackening Germany before the world could outdo the tale of burnings and beatings, of blackguardly assaults on defenceless and innocent people, which disgraced that country yesterday."[[12]](#cite_note-Gilbert41-12)

Estimates of fatalities caused by the attacks have varied. Early reports estimated that 91 Jews had been murdered.[[a]](#cite_note-13) Modern analysis of German scholarly sources puts the figure much higher; when deaths from post-arrest maltreatment and subsequent suicides are included, the death toll reaches the hundreds, with [Richard J. Evans](/wiki/Richard_J._Evans "Richard J. Evans") estimating 638 deaths by suicide,[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15) with a total between one and two thousand.[[8]](#cite_note-Evans-8)
Historians view *Kristallnacht* as a prelude to the [Final Solution](/wiki/Final_Solution "Final Solution") and the murder of six million Jews during [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust").[[15]](#cite_note-Hutchinson_1998-16)

In the 1920s, most German Jews were fully integrated into the country's society as citizens. They served in the army and navy and contributed to every field of German business, science and culture.[[16]](#cite_note-17) Conditions for German Jews began to worsen after the appointment of [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") (the Austrian-born leader of the [National Socialist German Workers' Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party") [abbreviated NSDAP in German]) as [Chancellor of Germany](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany "Chancellor of Germany") on 30 January 1933, and the [Enabling Act](/wiki/Enabling_Act_of_1933 "Enabling Act of 1933") (implemented 23 March 1933) which enabled the assumption of power by Hitler after the [Reichstag fire](/wiki/Reichstag_fire "Reichstag fire") of 27 February 1933.[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-19) From its inception, Hitler's regime moved quickly to introduce [anti-Jewish policies](/wiki/Racial_policy_of_Nazi_Germany "Racial policy of Nazi Germany"). [Nazi propaganda](/wiki/Nazi_propaganda "Nazi propaganda") alienated the 500,000 Jews living in Germany, who accounted for only 0.86% of the overall population, and framed them as an enemy responsible for Germany's defeat in the [First World War](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and for its subsequent economic disasters, such as the [1920s hyperinflation](/wiki/Inflation_in_the_Weimar_Republic "Inflation in the Weimar Republic") and the subsequent [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression").[[19]](#cite_note-Gilbert23-20) Beginning in 1933, the German government enacted a series of [anti-Jewish laws](/wiki/Anti-Jewish_legislation_in_prewar_Nazi_Germany "Anti-Jewish legislation in prewar Nazi Germany") restricting the rights of German Jews to earn a living, to enjoy full citizenship and to gain an education, including the *[Law for the Restoration of the Professional Civil Service](/wiki/Law_for_the_Restoration_of_the_Professional_Civil_Service "Law for the Restoration of the Professional Civil Service")* of 7 April 1933, which forbade Jews to work in the civil service.[[20]](#cite_note-21) The subsequent 1935 [Nuremberg Laws](/wiki/Nuremberg_Laws "Nuremberg Laws") stripped German Jews of their citizenship and prohibited Jews from marrying non-Jewish Germans.[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23)

These laws resulted in the exclusion and alienation of Jews from German social and political life.[[23]](#cite_note-24) Many sought asylum abroad; hundreds of thousands emigrated, but as [Chaim Weizmann](/wiki/Chaim_Weizmann "Chaim Weizmann") wrote in 1936, "The world seemed to be divided into two parts—those places where the Jews could not live and those where they could not enter."[[24]](#cite_note-25) The international [Évian Conference](/wiki/%C3%89vian_Conference "Évian Conference") on 6 July 1938 addressed the issue of Jewish and [Romani](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") immigration to other countries. By the time the conference took place, more than 250,000 Jews had fled Germany and Austria, which had been [annexed by Germany](/wiki/Anschluss "Anschluss") in March 1938; more than 300,000 German and Austrian Jews continued to seek refuge and asylum from oppression. As the number of Jews and Romani wanting to leave increased, the restrictions against them grew, with many countries tightening their rules for admission. By 1938, Germany "had entered a new radical phase in [anti-Semitic](/wiki/Anti-Semitic "Anti-Semitic") activity".[[25]](#cite_note-26) Some historians believe that the Nazi government had been contemplating a planned outbreak of violence against the Jews and were waiting for an appropriate provocation; there is evidence of this planning dating back to 1937.[[26]](#cite_note-27) In a 1997 interview, the German historian [Hans Mommsen](/wiki/Hans_Mommsen "Hans Mommsen") claimed that a major motive for the pogrom was the desire of the *[Gauleiters](/wiki/Gauleiter "Gauleiter")* of the NSDAP to seize Jewish property and [businesses](/wiki/Business "Business").[[27]](#cite_note-yadvashem1997-28) Mommsen stated:

> The need for money by the party organization stemmed from the fact that [Franz Xaver Schwarz](/wiki/Franz_Xaver_Schwarz "Franz Xaver Schwarz"), the party treasurer, kept the local and regional organizations of the party short of money. In the autumn of 1938, the increased pressure on Jewish property nourished the party's ambition, especially since [Hjalmar Schacht](/wiki/Hjalmar_Schacht "Hjalmar Schacht") had been ousted as *Reich* minister for economics. This, however, was only one aspect of the origin of the November 1938 pogrom, where the interests between the different agencies of party and state converged. While the Nazi party was interested in improving its financial strength on the regional and local level by taking over Jewish property, [Hermann Göring](/wiki/Hermann_G%C3%B6ring "Hermann Göring"), in charge of the [Four-Year Plan](/wiki/Four_Year_Plan "Four Year Plan"), hoped to acquire access to foreign currency in order to pay for the import of urgently-needed raw material. Heydrich and Himmler were interested in enforcing the emigration of German Jews.[[27]](#cite_note-yadvashem1997-28)

By 1938, Germany had entered a new radical phase in [anti-Semitic](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism") activity. Some historians believe that the Nazi government had been planning an outbreak of violence against the Jews since 1937 and were waiting for a suitable provocation.

### Expulsion of Polish Jews in Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kristallnacht&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Expulsion of Polish Jews in Germany")]

Main article: [1938 expulsion of Polish Jews from Germany](/wiki/1938_expulsion_of_Polish_Jews_from_Germany "1938 expulsion of Polish Jews from Germany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1982-174-27%2C_N%C3%BCrnberg%2C_Ausweisung_polnischer_Juden.jpg/330px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1982-174-27%2C_N%C3%BCrnberg%2C_Ausweisung_polnischer_Juden.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1982-174-27,_N%C3%BCrnberg,_Ausweisung_polnischer_Juden.jpg)

Polish Jews expelled from Germany in late October 1938

In August 1938, German authorities announced that residence permits for foreigners were being canceled and would have to be renewed.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This included German-born Jews of foreign citizenship. [Poland](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic") stated that it would renounce citizenship rights of [Polish Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Poland "History of the Jews in Poland") living abroad for at least five years after the end of October, effectively making them stateless.[[28]](#cite_note-29) In the so-called "*[Polenaktion](/wiki/Polenaktion "Polenaktion")*", more than 12,000 Polish Jews, among them the philosopher and theologian Rabbi [Abraham Joshua Heschel](/wiki/Abraham_Joshua_Heschel "Abraham Joshua Heschel") and future literary critic [Marcel Reich-Ranicki](/wiki/Marcel_Reich-Ranicki "Marcel Reich-Ranicki"), were expelled from Germany on 28 October 1938, on Hitler's orders. They were ordered to leave their homes in a single night and were allowed only one suitcase per person to carry their belongings. As the Jews were taken away, their remaining possessions were seized as loot both by Nazi authorities and by neighbors.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The deportees were taken from their homes to railway stations and were put on trains to the Polish border, where Polish border guards sent them back into Germany. This stalemate continued for days in the pouring rain, with the Jews marching without food or shelter between the borders.[[29]](#cite_note-30) Four thousand were granted entry into Poland, but the remaining 8,000 were forced to stay at the border. They waited there in harsh conditions to be allowed to enter Poland. A British newspaper told its readers that hundreds "are reported to be lying about, penniless and deserted, in little villages along the frontier near where they had been driven out by the Gestapo and left."[[30]](#cite_note-31) Conditions in the [refugee camps](/wiki/Refugee_camps "Refugee camps") "were so bad that some actually tried to escape back into Germany and were shot", recalled a British woman who was sent to help those who had been expelled.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

### Shooting of vom Rath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kristallnacht&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Shooting of vom Rath")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1988-078-07%2C_Herschel_Feibel_Grynszpan.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1988-078-07%2C_Herschel_Feibel_Grynszpan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1988-078-07,_Herschel_Feibel_Grynszpan.jpg)

[Herschel Grynszpan](/wiki/Herschel_Grynszpan "Herschel Grynszpan"), 7 November 1938

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Ernst-vom-Rath.jpg/250px-Ernst-vom-Rath.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ernst-vom-Rath.jpg)

[Ernst vom Rath](/wiki/Ernst_vom_Rath "Ernst vom Rath")

Among those expelled was the family of Sendel and Riva Grynszpan, Polish Jews who had emigrated to Germany in 1911 and settled in [Hanover](/wiki/Hanover "Hanover"), Germany. At the trial of [Adolf Eichmann](/wiki/Adolf_Eichmann "Adolf Eichmann") in 1961, Sendel Grynszpan recounted the events of their deportation from Hanover on the night of 27 October 1938: "Then they took us in police trucks, in prisoners' lorries, about 20 men in each truck, and they took us to the railway station. The streets were full of people shouting: '*Juden Raus! Auf Nach Palästina!*'" ("Jews get out! Go to Palestine!").[[32]](#cite_note-33) Their seventeen-year-old son [Herschel](/wiki/Herschel_Grynszpan "Herschel Grynszpan") was living in Paris with an uncle.[[15]](#cite_note-Hutchinson_1998-16) Herschel received a postcard from his family from the Polish border, describing the family's expulsion: "No one told us what was up, but we realized this was going to be the end .... We don't have a penny. Could you send us something?"[[33]](#cite_note-GSA-34) He received the postcard on 3 November 1938.

On the morning of Monday, 7 November 1938, he purchased a revolver and a box of bullets, then went to the German embassy and asked to see an embassy official. After he was taken to the office of German diplomat [Ernst vom Rath](/wiki/Ernst_vom_Rath "Ernst vom Rath"), Grynszpan fired five bullets at vom Rath, two of which hit him in the abdomen. Vom Rath was a professional diplomat with the Foreign Office who expressed anti-Nazi sympathies, largely based on the Nazis' treatment of the Jews and was under Gestapo investigation for being politically unreliable.[[34]](#cite_note-35) However, he also argued that the anti-Semitic laws were "necessary" to allow the *[Volksgemeinschaft](/wiki/Volksgemeinschaft "Volksgemeinschaft")* to flourish.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

Grynszpan made no attempt to escape the French police and freely confessed to the shooting. He stated that his motives were to avenge the Jewish people for the actions taken by the Germans against Jews. In his pocket, he carried a postcard to his parents with the message, "With God's help. My dear parents, I could not do otherwise, may God forgive me, the heart bleeds when I hear of your tragedy and that of the 12,000 Jews. I must protest so that the whole world hears my protest, and that I will do. Forgive me."[[36]](#cite_note-schwab-37)

The next day, the German government retaliated, barring Jewish children from German state elementary schools, indefinitely suspending Jewish cultural activities, and putting a halt to the publication of Jewish newspapers and magazines, including the three national German Jewish newspapers. A newspaper in Britain described the last move, which cut off the Jewish populace from their leaders, as "intended to disrupt the Jewish community and rob it of the last frail ties which hold it together."[[19]](#cite_note-Gilbert23-20) Their rights as citizens had been stripped.[[37]](#cite_note-38) One of the first legal measures issued was an order by Heinrich Himmler, commander of all German police, forbidding Jews to possess any weapons whatsoever and imposing a penalty of twenty years' confinement in a concentration camp upon every Jew found in possession of a weapon hereafter.[[38]](#cite_note-NYT1938-39)

## Pogrom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kristallnacht&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Pogrom")]

### Death of Ernst vom Rath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kristallnacht&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Death of Ernst vom Rath")]

[Ernst vom Rath](/wiki/Ernst_vom_Rath "Ernst vom Rath") died of his wounds on 9 November 1938. Word of his death reached Hitler that evening while he was with several key members of the Nazi party at a dinner commemorating the 1923 [Beer Hall Putsch](/wiki/Beer_Hall_Putsch "Beer Hall Putsch"). After intense discussions, Hitler left the assembly abruptly without giving his usual address. Propaganda Minister [Joseph Goebbels](/wiki/Joseph_Goebbels "Joseph Goebbels") delivered the speech in his place, and said that "the Führer has decided that... demonstrations should not be prepared or organized by the party, but insofar as they erupt spontaneously, they are not to be hampered."[[39]](#cite_note-40) The chief party judge [Walter Buch](/wiki/Walter_Buch "Walter Buch") later stated that the message was clear; with these words, Goebbels had commanded the party leaders to organize a pogrom.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

Some leading party officials disagreed with Goebbels' actions, fearing the diplomatic crisis it would provoke. [Heinrich Himmler](/wiki/Heinrich_Himmler "Heinrich Himmler") wrote, "I suppose that it is Goebbels's megalomania ... and stupidity which is responsible for starting this operation now, in a particularly difficult diplomatic situation."[[41]](#cite_note-42) The Israeli historian [Saul Friedländer](/wiki/Saul_Friedl%C3%A4nder "Saul Friedländer") believes that Goebbels had personal reasons for wanting to bring about *Kristallnacht*. Goebbels had recently suffered humiliation for the ineffectiveness of his propaganda campaign during the [Sudeten crisis](/wiki/Sudetenland_Crisis "Sudetenland Crisis"), and was in some disgrace over an affair with a [Czech](/wiki/Czechs "Czechs") actress, [Lída Baarová](/wiki/L%C3%ADda_Baarov%C3%A1 "Lída Baarová"). Goebbels needed a chance to improve his standing in the eyes of Hitler. At 1:20 a.m. on 10 November 1938, [Reinhard Heydrich](/wiki/Reinhard_Heydrich "Reinhard Heydrich") sent an urgent secret telegram to the *[Sicherheitspolizei](/wiki/Sicherheitspolizei "Sicherheitspolizei")* (Security Police; SiPo) and the *[Sturmabteilung](/wiki/Sturmabteilung "Sturmabteilung")* (SA), containing instructions regarding the riots. This included guidelines for the protection of foreigners and non-Jewish businesses and property. Police were instructed not to interfere with the riots unless the guidelines were violated. Police were also instructed to seize Jewish archives from synagogues and community offices, and to arrest and detain "healthy male Jews, who are not too old", for eventual transfer to (labor) concentration camps.[[42]](#cite_note-43) [Heinrich Müller](/wiki/Heinrich_M%C3%BCller_(Gestapo) "Heinrich Müller (Gestapo)"), in a message to SA and SS commanders, stated the "most extreme measures" were to be taken against Jewish people.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

### Riots and Kristallnacht

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kristallnacht&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Riots and  Kristallnacht")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1970-083-42%2C_Magdeburg%2C_zerst%C3%B6rtes_j%C3%BCdisches_Gesch%C3%A4ft.jpg/330px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1970-083-42%2C_Magdeburg%2C_zerst%C3%B6rtes_j%C3%BCdisches_Gesch%C3%A4ft.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1970-083-42,_Magdeburg,_zerst%C3%B6rtes_j%C3%BCdisches_Gesch%C3%A4ft.jpg)

*Kristallnacht*, shop damage in [Magdeburg](/wiki/Magdeburg "Magdeburg")

Beginning on 9 November, the SA and Hitler Youth shattered the windows of about 7,500 Jewish stores and businesses, hence the name *Kristallnacht* (Crystal Night), and looted their goods.[[44]](#cite_note-45)[[45]](#cite_note-ushmm-46) Jewish homes were ransacked all throughout Germany. Although violence against Jews had not been explicitly condoned by the authorities, there were cases of Jews being beaten or assaulted. Following the violence, police departments recorded a large number of suicides and rapes.[[45]](#cite_note-ushmm-46)

The rioters destroyed many hundreds of synagogues throughout Germany, Austria, and the Sudetenland, with estimates of over [1,668 synagogues and prayer rooms ransacked](https://www.sources.com/SSR/Docs/SSRW-Kristallnacht.htm),[[46]](#cite_note-47) [and 95 destroyed](https://www.sources.com/SSR/Docs/SSRW-Kristallnacht.htm). Many Jewish cemeteries, more than 7,000 Jewish shops, and 29 department stores were damaged, and in many cases destroyed. 91 Jews were murdered and [more than 30,000 Jewish men were arrested](/wiki/Mass_arrests_after_Kristallnacht "Mass arrests after Kristallnacht") and imprisoned in Nazi concentration camps, primarily [Dachau](/wiki/Dachau_concentration_camp "Dachau concentration camp"), [Buchenwald](/wiki/Buchenwald_concentration_camp "Buchenwald concentration camp"), and [Sachsenhausen](/wiki/Sachsenhausen_concentration_camp "Sachsenhausen concentration camp").[[47]](#cite_note-48)

The synagogues, some centuries old, were also victims of considerable violence and vandalism, with the tactics the Stormtroopers practiced on these and other sacred sites described as "approaching the ghoulish" by the United States Consul in Leipzig. Tombstones were uprooted and graves violated. Fires were lit, and prayer books, scrolls, artwork and philosophy texts were thrown upon them, and precious buildings were either burned or smashed until unrecognizable. Eric Lucas recalls the destruction of the synagogue that a tiny Jewish community had constructed in a small village only twelve years earlier:

> It did not take long before the first heavy grey stones came tumbling down, and the children of the village amused themselves as they flung stones into the many colored windows. When the first rays of a cold and pale November sun penetrated the heavy dark clouds, the little synagogue was but a heap of stone, broken glass and smashed-up woodwork.[[48]](#cite_note-49)

The *Daily Telegraph* correspondent, [Hugh Greene](/wiki/Hugh_Greene "Hugh Greene"), wrote of events in Berlin:

> Mob law ruled in Berlin throughout the afternoon and evening and hordes of hooligans indulged in an orgy of destruction. I have seen several anti-Jewish outbreaks in Germany during the last five years, but never anything as nauseating as this. Racial hatred and hysteria seemed to have taken complete hold of otherwise decent people. I saw fashionably dressed women clapping their hands and screaming with glee, while respectable middle-class mothers held up their babies to see the 'fun'.[[49]](#cite_note-Carleton_Greene-50)

Many Berliners were, however, deeply ashamed of the pogrom, and some took great personal risks to offer help to their beleaguered Jewish neighbors. The son of a US consular official heard the janitor of his block cry: "They must have emptied the insane asylums and penitentiaries to find people who'd do things like that!"[[49]](#cite_note-Carleton_Greene-50)

[KOLD](/wiki/KOLD-TV "KOLD-TV") briefly reported on a 2008 remembrance meeting at a local Jewish congregation. According to eyewitness Esther Harris: "They ripped up the belongings, the books, knocked over furniture, shouted obscenities".[[50]](#cite_note-kold-51) Historian [Gerhard Weinberg](/wiki/Gerhard_Weinberg "Gerhard Weinberg") is quoted as saying:

> Houses of worship burned down, vandalized, in every community in the country where people either participate or watch.[[50]](#cite_note-kold-51)

## Aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kristallnacht&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Aftermath")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Destroyed_Ohel_Yaaqov_Synagogue.jpeg/330px-Destroyed_Ohel_Yaaqov_Synagogue.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Destroyed_Ohel_Yaaqov_Synagogue.jpeg)

A ruined synagogue in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich") after *Kristallnacht*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Synagogue_Eisenach_burning-_Nov_1938.jpg/330px-Synagogue_Eisenach_burning-_Nov_1938.jpg)](/wiki/File:Synagogue_Eisenach_burning-_Nov_1938.jpg)

A ruined synagogue in [Eisenach](/wiki/Eisenach "Eisenach") after *Kristallnacht*

The former [German Emperor](/wiki/German_Emperor "German Emperor") [Wilhelm II](/wiki/Wilhelm_II "Wilhelm II") commented "For the first time, I am ashamed to be German."[[51]](#cite_note-52)

Göring, who was in favor of expropriating the property of the Jews rather than destroying as had happened in the pogrom, directly complained to *Sicherheitspolizei* Chief Heydrich immediately after the events: "I'd rather you had beaten to death two-hundred Jews than destroy so many valuable assets!" (*"Mir wäre lieber gewesen, ihr hättet 200 Juden erschlagen und hättet nicht solche Werte vernichtet!"*).[[52]](#cite_note-53) Göring met with other members of the Nazi leadership on 12 November to plan the next steps after the riot, setting the stage for formal government action. In the transcript of the meeting, Göring said,

> I have received a letter written on the [Führer](/wiki/F%C3%BChrer "Führer")'s orders requesting that the Jewish question be now, once and for all, coordinated and solved one way or another .... I should not want to leave any doubt, gentlemen, as to the aim of today's meeting. We have not come together merely to talk again, but to make decisions, and I implore competent agencies to take all measures for the elimination of the Jew from the German economy, and to submit them to me.[[53]](#cite_note-54)

The persecution and economic damage inflicted upon German Jews continued after the pogrom, even as their places of business were ransacked. They were forced to pay *[Judenvermögensabgabe](/wiki/Judenverm%C3%B6gensabgabe "Judenvermögensabgabe")*, a collective fine or "atonement contribution" of one billion Reichsmarks for the murder of vom Rath (equivalent to €4 billion 2021 or 7 billion in 2020 USD), which was levied by the compulsory acquisition of 20% of all Jewish property by the state. Six million Reichsmarks of insurance payments for property damage due to the Jewish community were instead paid to the Reich government as "damages to the German Nation". Jews were required to pay for the cost of all damages caused by the pogrom to their residences and businesses.[[54]](#cite_note-55)[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUllrich2016678-57)

The number of emigrating Jews surged, as those who were able to leave abandoned the country. In the ten months following *Kristallnacht*, more than 115,000 Jews emigrated from the Reich.[[57]](#cite_note-58) The majority went to other European countries, the United States or [Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine"), though at least 14,000 made it to [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai_Ghetto "Shanghai Ghetto"), [China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)"). As part of government policy, the Nazis seized houses, shops, and other property the émigrés left behind. Many of the destroyed remains of Jewish property plundered during Kristallnacht were dumped near [Brandenburg](/wiki/Brandenburg "Brandenburg"). In October 2008, this dumpsite was discovered by [Yaron Svoray](/wiki/Yaron_Svoray "Yaron Svoray"), an investigative journalist. The site, the size of four football fields, contained an extensive array of personal and ceremonial items looted during the riots against Jewish property and places of worship on the night of 9 November 1938. It is believed the goods were brought by rail to the outskirts of the village and dumped on designated land. Among the items found were glass bottles engraved with the [Star of David](/wiki/Star_of_David "Star of David"), [mezuzot](/wiki/Mezuzot "Mezuzot"), painted window sills, and the armrests of chairs found in synagogues, in addition to an ornamental swastika.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

## Responses to *Kristallnacht*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kristallnacht&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Responses to Kristallnacht")]

### In Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kristallnacht&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: In Germany")]

The reaction of non-Jewish Germans to *Kristallnacht* was varied. Many spectators gathered on the scenes, most of them in silence. The local fire departments confined themselves to preventing the flames from spreading to neighboring buildings. In Berlin, police Lieutenant Otto Bellgardt prevented SA troopers from setting the [New Synagogue](/wiki/New_Synagogue,_Berlin "New Synagogue, Berlin") on fire, earning his superior officer a verbal reprimand from the commissioner.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Paul_Ehrlich_by_Franz_Wilhelm_Voigt_2014.031_crop.jpg/250px-Paul_Ehrlich_by_Franz_Wilhelm_Voigt_2014.031_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paul_Ehrlich_by_Franz_Wilhelm_Voigt_2014.031_crop.jpg)

*Portrait of [Paul Ehrlich](/wiki/Paul_Ehrlich "Paul Ehrlich")*, damaged on *Kristallnacht*, then restored by a German neighbor

The British historian [Martin Gilbert](/wiki/Martin_Gilbert "Martin Gilbert") believes that "many non-Jews resented the round-up",[[60]](#cite_note-61) his opinion being supported by German witness Dr. Arthur Flehinger who recalls seeing "people crying while watching from behind their curtains".[[61]](#cite_note-62) Rolf Dessauer recalls how a neighbor came forward and restored a portrait of [Paul Ehrlich](/wiki/Paul_Ehrlich "Paul Ehrlich") that had been "slashed to ribbons" by the *Sturmabteilung*. "He wanted it to be known that not all Germans supported Kristallnacht."[[62]](#cite_note-Distillations-63)

The extent of the damage done on Kristallnacht was so great that many Germans are said to have expressed their disapproval of it, and to have described it as senseless.[[63]](#cite_note-64) There was, however, no personal comment or even acknowledgment from the German leader [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") himself about *Kristallnacht*.[[64]](#cite_note-davidson-65)

In an article released for publication on the evening of 11 November, Goebbels ascribed the events of *Kristallnacht* to the "healthy instincts" of the German people. He went on to explain: "The German people are anti-Semitic. It has no desire to have its rights restricted or to be provoked in the future by parasites of the Jewish race."[[65]](#cite_note-66) Less than 24 hours after Kristallnacht, Adolf Hitler made a one-hour-long speech in front of a group of journalists where he completely ignored the prior evening's events. According to Eugene Davidson, the reason for this was that Hitler wished to avoid being directly connected to an event that he knew many of those present condemned, regardless of Goebbels's unconvincing explanation that Kristallnacht was caused by popular wrath.[[64]](#cite_note-davidson-65) Goebbels met the foreign press in the afternoon of 11 November and said that the burning of synagogues and damage to Jewish owned property had been "spontaneous manifestations of indignation against the murder of Herr Vom Rath by the young Jew Grynsban [sic]".[[66]](#cite_note-67)

In 1938, just after Kristallnacht, the psychologist Michael Müller-Claudius interviewed 41 randomly selected Nazi Party members on their attitudes towards racial persecution. Of the interviewed party members, 63% expressed extreme indignation against it, 5% expressed approval, and the remaining 32% were noncommittal.[[67]](#cite_note-68) A study conducted in 1933 had then shown that 33% of Nazi Party members held no racial prejudice, while 13% supported persecution[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. Sarah Ann Gordon sees two possible reasons for this difference. First, by 1938, large numbers of Germans had joined the Nazi Party for pragmatic reasons rather than ideology, thus diluting the percentage of rabid antisemites; second, the Kristallnacht could have caused party members to reject antisemitism that had been acceptable to them in abstract terms but which they could not support when they saw it concretely enacted.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1984263–264-69) During the events of Kristallnacht, several [Gauleiter](/wiki/Gauleiter "Gauleiter") and deputy Gauleiters had refused orders to enact the Kristallnacht, and many leaders of the SA and of the Hitler Youth also openly refused party orders, while expressing disgust.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1984266-70) Some Nazis helped Jews during the Kristallnacht.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1984266-70)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Plaque_on_the_New_Synagogue.JPG/330px-Plaque_on_the_New_Synagogue.JPG)](/wiki/File:Plaque_on_the_New_Synagogue.JPG)

After 1945 some synagogues were restored. This one in Berlin features a plaque, reading "Never forget", a common expression around Berlin.

As it was aware that the German public did not support the Kristallnacht, the propaganda ministry directed the German press to portray opponents of racial persecution as disloyal.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1984159-71) The press was also under orders to downplay the Kristallnacht, describing general events at the local level only, with prohibition against depictions of individual events.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1984156-72) In 1939 this was extended to a prohibition on reporting any anti-Jewish measures.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1984157-73)

The U.S. ambassador to Germany reported:

> In view of this being a totalitarian state a surprising characteristic of the situation here is the intensity and scope among German citizens of condemnation of the recent happenings against Jews.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1984176-74)

To the consternation of the Nazis, the Kristallnacht affected public opinion counter to their desires, spurring the peak of opposition to Nazi racial policies; according to almost all accounts, the vast majority of Germans rejected the violence perpetrated against the Jews.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1984180,_207-75) Verbal complaints grew rapidly, with the Düsseldorf branch of the Gestapo reporting a sharp decline in anti-Semitic attitudes among the population.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1984175–179,_215-76)

There are many indications of Protestant and Catholic disapproval of racial persecution; for example, anti-Nazi Protestants adopted the [Barmen Declaration](/wiki/Barmen_Declaration "Barmen Declaration") in 1934, the Catholic church had already distributed [pastoral letters](/wiki/Pastoral_letters "Pastoral letters") critical of Nazi racial ideology, and the Nazi regime expected to encounter organised resistance from these parties following Kristallnacht.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1984251,_252,_258,_259-77) The Catholic leadership, however, just as the various Protestant churches, refrained from responding with organised action.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon1984251,_252,_258,_259-77)

Martin Sasse, Nazi Party member and bishop of the [Evangelical Lutheran Church in Thuringia](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_in_Thuringia "Evangelical Lutheran Church in Thuringia"), leading member of the Nazi [German Christians](/wiki/German_Christians_(movement) "German Christians (movement)")−one of the schismatic factions of German Protestantism−published a compendium of [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther")'s writings shortly after the *Kristallnacht*. He "applauded the burning of the synagogues" and the coincidence of the day, writing in the introduction, "On 10 November 1938, on Luther's birthday, the synagogues are burning in Germany." The German people, he urged, ought to heed these words "of the greatest anti-Semite of his time, the warner of his people against the Jews."[[77]](#cite_note-78) British theologian [Diarmaid MacCulloch](/wiki/Diarmaid_MacCulloch "Diarmaid MacCulloch") wrote in 2004 that Luther's 1543 pamphlet, "[On the Jews and Their Lies](/wiki/On_the_Jews_and_Their_Lies "On the Jews and Their Lies")" was a "blueprint" for the *Kristallnacht*.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

### Internationally

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kristallnacht&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Internationally")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/British_Jews_protest_against_immigration_restrictions_to_Palestine_after_Kristallnacht.jpg/250px-British_Jews_protest_against_immigration_restrictions_to_Palestine_after_Kristallnacht.jpg)](/wiki/File:British_Jews_protest_against_immigration_restrictions_to_Palestine_after_Kristallnacht.jpg)

British Jews protest against immigration restrictions to Palestine after Kristallnacht, November 1938.

*Kristallnacht* sparked international outrage. According to [Volker Ullrich](/wiki/Volker_Ullrich "Volker Ullrich"), "a line had been crossed: Germany had left the community of civilised nations."[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUllrich2016676-80) It discredited pro-Nazi movements in Europe and [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), leading to a sharp decline in their support. Many newspapers condemned *Kristallnacht*, with some of them comparing it to the [murderous pogroms incited by Imperial Russia](/wiki/Anti-Jewish_pogroms_in_the_Russian_Empire "Anti-Jewish pogroms in the Russian Empire"). The United States recalled its ambassador Hugh Wilson, but it did not break off diplomatic relations, while other governments severed diplomatic relations with Germany in protest. The British government approved the [Kindertransport](/wiki/Kindertransport "Kindertransport") program for refugee children.

The pogrom marked a turning point in relations between Nazi Germany and the rest of the world. The brutality of *Kristallnacht*, and the Nazi government's deliberate policy of encouraging the violence once it had begun, laid bare the repressive nature and widespread anti-Semitism entrenched in Germany. World opinion thus turned sharply against the Nazi regime, with some politicians calling for war. On 6 December 1938, [William Cooper](/wiki/William_Cooper_(Aboriginal_Australian) "William Cooper (Aboriginal Australian)"), an Aboriginal Australian, led a delegation of the [Australian Aboriginal League](/wiki/Australian_Aboriginal_League "Australian Aboriginal League") on a march through [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne") to the German Consulate to deliver a petition which condemned the "cruel persecution of the Jewish people by the Nazi government of Germany". German officials refused to accept the tendered document.[[80]](#cite_note-81)

In Spain, as the [civil war](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") was raging on, reactions were mixed on both sides. The [Nationalist](/wiki/Nationalist_faction_(Spanish_Civil_War) "Nationalist faction (Spanish Civil War)")-controlled press celebrated *Kristallnacht*, repeating the Nazi version of the events and justifying the events [from an antisemitic, Catholic perspective](/wiki/Antisemitism_in_Christianity "Antisemitism in Christianity"). Meanwhile, the [Republican](/wiki/Republican_faction_(Spanish_Civil_War) "Republican faction (Spanish Civil War)")-controlled ones condemned it, while the Republican government offered refugee status to people suffering persecution from the Nazi regime.[[81]](#cite_note-82)

After *Kristallnacht*, [Salvador Allende](/wiki/Salvador_Allende "Salvador Allende"), [Gabriel González Videla](/wiki/Gabriel_Gonz%C3%A1lez_Videla "Gabriel González Videla"), [Marmaduke Grove](/wiki/Marmaduke_Grove "Marmaduke Grove"), [Florencio Durán](/wiki/Florencio_Dur%C3%A1n "Florencio Durán") and other members of the [Congress of Chile](/wiki/National_Congress_of_Chile "National Congress of Chile") sent a telegram to Adolf Hitler denouncing the persecution of Jews.[[82]](#cite_note-83)
A more personal response, in 1939, was the oratorio *[A Child of Our Time](/wiki/A_Child_of_Our_Time "A Child of Our Time")* by the English composer [Michael Tippett](/wiki/Michael_Tippett "Michael Tippett").[[83]](#cite_note-84) Once the [government of Sweden](/wiki/Government_of_Sweden "Government of Sweden") was informed of *Kristallnacht*, it successfully demanded the Nazi authorities stamp the letter [J](/wiki/J "J") in red ink on passports of German Jews to make it easier for Swedish border officials to turn them away.[[84]](#cite_note-85)

### Post-war trials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kristallnacht&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Post-war trials")]

After the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), there were hundreds of trials over Kristallnacht. The trials were conducted exclusively by German and Austrian courts; the Allied occupation authorities did not have jurisdiction since none of the victims were Allied nationals.[[85]](#cite_note-86)

## *Kristallnacht* as a turning point

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kristallnacht&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Kristallnacht as a turning point")]

*Kristallnacht* changed the nature of Nazi Germany's persecution of the Jews from economic, political, and social exclusion to physical violence, including beatings, incarceration, and murder; the event is often referred to as the beginning of [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust"). In this view, it is not only described as a pogrom, it is also described as a critical stage within a process in which each step becomes the seed of the next step.[[86]](#cite_note-:0-87) An account cited that Hitler's green light for *Kristallnacht* was made with the belief that it would help him realize his ambition of getting rid of the Jews in Germany.[[86]](#cite_note-:0-87) Prior to this large-scale and organized violence against the Jews, the Nazis' primary objective was to eject them from Germany, leaving their wealth behind.[[86]](#cite_note-:0-87) In the words of historian Max Rein in 1988, "*Kristallnacht* came ... and everything was changed."[[87]](#cite_note-Krefeld-88)

While November 1938 predated the overt articulation of "the [Final Solution](/wiki/Final_Solution "Final Solution")", it foreshadowed the [genocide](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") to come. Around the time of *Kristallnacht*, the [SS](/wiki/Schutzstaffel "Schutzstaffel") newspaper *[Das Schwarze Korps](/wiki/Das_Schwarze_Korps "Das Schwarze Korps")* called for a "destruction by swords and flames." At a conference on the day after the pogrom, [Hermann Göring](/wiki/Hermann_G%C3%B6ring "Hermann Göring") said: "The Jewish problem will reach its solution if, in anytime soon, we will be drawn into war beyond our border—then it is obvious that we will have to manage a final account with the Jews."[[19]](#cite_note-Gilbert23-20)

*Kristallnacht* was also instrumental in changing global public opinion. In the United States, for instance, it was this specific incident that came to symbolize [Nazism](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism"), forging the association between National Socialism and [evil](/wiki/Evil "Evil").[[88]](#cite_note-89)

## Modern references

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kristallnacht&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Modern references")]

Five decades later, [9 November](/wiki/9_November_in_German_history "9 November in German history")'s association with the anniversary of *Kristallnacht*, as well as the earlier [Beer Hall Putsch](/wiki/Beer_Hall_Putsch "Beer Hall Putsch"), was cited as the main reason as to why *Schicksalstag*, the day the [Berlin Wall](/wiki/Berlin_Wall "Berlin Wall") came down in 1989, was not turned into a new German national [holiday](/wiki/Public_holiday "Public holiday"); a different day was chosen (3 October 1990, [German reunification](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification")).[[89]](#cite_note-90)

The *[avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde")* guitarist [Gary Lucas](/wiki/Gary_Lucas "Gary Lucas")'s 1988 composition "Verklärte Kristallnacht", which juxtaposes what would become the Israeli national anthem ten years after *Kristallnacht*, "[Hatikvah](/wiki/Hatikvah "Hatikvah")", with phrases from the German national anthem "[Deutschland Über Alles](/wiki/Deutschlandlied "Deutschlandlied")" amid wild electronic shrieks and noise, is intended to be a sonic representation of the horrors of *Kristallnacht*. It was premiered at the 1988 [Berlin Jazz Festival](/wiki/JazzFest_Berlin "JazzFest Berlin") and received rave reviews. (The title is a reference to [Arnold Schoenberg](/wiki/Arnold_Schoenberg "Arnold Schoenberg")'s 1899 work *[Verklärte Nacht](/wiki/Verkl%C3%A4rte_Nacht "Verklärte Nacht")* that presaged his pioneering work on [atonal music](/wiki/Atonality "Atonality"); Schoenberg was an Austrian Jew who would move to the United States to escape the Nazis).[[90]](#cite_note-91)

In 1989, [Al Gore](/wiki/Al_Gore "Al Gore"), then a senator from [Tennessee](/wiki/Tennessee "Tennessee") and later [Vice President of the United States](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_United_States "Vice President of the United States"), wrote of an "ecological Kristallnacht" in *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*. He opined that events which were then taking place, such as [deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation") and [ozone depletion](/wiki/Ozone_depletion "Ozone depletion"), prefigured a greater environmental catastrophe in the same way that *Kristallnacht* prefigured the Holocaust.[[91]](#cite_note-92)

*Kristallnacht* was the inspiration for the 1993 album *[Kristallnacht](/wiki/Kristallnacht_(album) "Kristallnacht (album)")* by the composer [John Zorn](/wiki/John_Zorn "John Zorn"). The German [power metal](/wiki/Power_metal "Power metal") band [Masterplan](/wiki/Masterplan_(band) "Masterplan (band)")'s debut album, *[Masterplan](/wiki/Masterplan_(Masterplan_album) "Masterplan (Masterplan album)")* (2003), features an anti-Nazi song entitled "Crystal Night" as the fourth track. The German band [BAP](/wiki/BAP_(German_band) "BAP (German band)") published a song titled "[Kristallnaach](/wiki/Kristallnaach "Kristallnaach")" in their [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne") [dialect](/wiki/Colognian_dialect "Colognian dialect"), dealing with the emotions engendered by the *Kristallnacht*.[[92]](#cite_note-93)

*Kristallnacht* was the inspiration for the 1988 composition *Mayn Yngele* by the composer [Frederic Rzewski](/wiki/Frederic_Rzewski "Frederic Rzewski"), of which he says: "I began writing this piece in November 1988, on the 50th anniversary of *Kristallnacht* .... My piece is a reflection on that vanished part of Jewish tradition which so strongly colors, by its absence, the culture of our time".[[93]](#cite_note-94)

In 2014, *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")* published a letter from billionaire [Thomas Perkins](/wiki/Thomas_Perkins_(businessman) "Thomas Perkins (businessman)") that compared the "progressive war on the American one percent" of wealthiest Americans and the [Occupy movement](/wiki/Occupy_movement "Occupy movement")'s "demonization of the rich" to *Kristallnacht* and [antisemitism](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism") in [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany").[[94]](#cite_note-Kristallnacht-95) The letter was widely criticized and condemned in *[The Atlantic](/wiki/The_Atlantic "The Atlantic")*,[[95]](#cite_note-96) *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent "The Independent")*,[[96]](#cite_note-97) among bloggers, Twitter users, and "his own colleagues in [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley")".[[97]](#cite_note-ross-98) Perkins subsequently apologized for making the comparisons with Nazi Germany, but otherwise stood by his letter, saying, "In the Nazi era it was racial demonization, now it's class demonization."[[97]](#cite_note-ross-98)

*Kristallnacht* has been referenced both explicitly and implicitly in countless cases of vandalism of Jewish property including the toppling of gravestones in a Jewish cemetery in suburban St. Louis, Missouri,[[98]](#cite_note-99) and the two 2017 vandalisms of the [New England Holocaust Memorial](/wiki/New_England_Holocaust_Memorial "New England Holocaust Memorial"), as the memorial's founder Steve Ross discusses in his book, *From Broken Glass: My Story of Finding Hope in Hitler's Death Camps to Inspire a New Generation*.[[99]](#cite_note-100) The Sri Lankan Finance Minister [Mangala Samaraweera](/wiki/Mangala_Samaraweera "Mangala Samaraweera") also used the term to describe the violence in 2019 against Muslims by Sinhalese nationalists.[[100]](#cite_note-101)

On 10 January 2021, former Governor of [California](/wiki/California "California") [Arnold Schwarzenegger](/wiki/Arnold_Schwarzenegger "Arnold Schwarzenegger") gave a speech claiming that President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") had incited the [attack on the U.S. Capitol](/wiki/January_6_United_States_Capitol_attack "January 6 United States Capitol attack") on 6 January, comparing the event to Kristallnacht.[[101]](#cite_note-102)[[102]](#cite_note-103)

On 9 November 2022, [KFC](/wiki/KFC "KFC") app users in Germany were sent a message reading "It's memorial day for *Kristallnacht*! Treat yourself with more tender cheese on your crispy chicken." KFC issued an apology approximately an hour later, blaming the original message on an "error in our system".[[103]](#cite_note-104)

The [*Fortnite* Holocaust Museum](/wiki/Fortnite_Holocaust_Museum "Fortnite Holocaust Museum"), a virtual museum based inside the videogame *[Fortnite](/wiki/Fortnite "Fortnite")*, is set to feature a display featuring the Kristallnacht.[[104]](#cite_note-:1-105)

On 9 November 2024, the *Kristallnacht* anniversary, the only [glatt kosher](/wiki/Glatt_kosher "Glatt kosher") restaurant in Washington, D.C. had its windows smashed.[[105]](#cite_note-d320-106)

The [November 2024 Amsterdam riots](/wiki/November_2024_Amsterdam_riots "November 2024 Amsterdam riots") in the Netherlands have been compared by Israeli and pro-Israeli organizations to *Kristallnacht*. On 7 November 2024, Jewish and Israeli supporters of the [Maccabi Tel Aviv](/wiki/Maccabi_Tel_Aviv "Maccabi Tel Aviv") football team were attacked by pro-Palestinian groups and local football attendees following that day's [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") match.[[106]](#cite_note-107) The Amsterdam attacks were internationally condemned. Several Jewish advocacy groups, such as Israeli NGO [Combat Antisemitism Movement](/wiki/Combat_Antisemitism_Movement "Combat Antisemitism Movement"), the Orthodox Union, and the Jewish Federation, have called the November 2024 Amsterdam attacks a "modern-day *Kristallnacht*".[[107]](#cite_note-108) Israeli Prime Minister [Benjamin Netanyahu](/wiki/Benjamin_Netanyahu "Benjamin Netanyahu") condemned the attacks, noting that it took place just before the 86th anniversary of *Kristallnacht* and bore many similarities.[[108]](#cite_note-l161-109) Other organizations described this characterization as one-sided, accusing Maccabi fans of pulling [down Palestinian flags](/wiki/Flag_of_Palestine "Flag of Palestine") from houses, making racist [anti-Arab chants](/wiki/Anti-Arab_racism "Anti-Arab racism") such as "[death to Arabs](/wiki/Death_to_Arabs "Death to Arabs")", assaulting people, and vandalizing local property.[[109]](#cite_note-110)[[110]](#cite_note-AA-1117-111)[[111]](#cite_note-:18-112) These claims have since been called into question. The [German Foreign Minister, Annalena Baerbock](/wiki/Annalena_Baerbock "Annalena Baerbock") wrote on X:[[112]](#cite_note-113) “The images out of Amsterdam are awful and deeply shameful. The outbreak of such violence against Jews breaks all boundaries. There is no justification for this.”

In 2025 a feature film titled [*Kristallnacht*](/wiki/Kristallnacht_(film) "Kristallnacht (film)") directed by [Stefan Ruzowitzky](/wiki/Stefan_Ruzowitzky "Stefan Ruzowitzky") about the historical events was in production in Austria on the 80th anniversary of the Nuremberg Trials. *Kristallnacht* was also mentioned in the 2025 Sky Original Film [Nuremberg](/wiki/Nuremberg_(2025_film) "Nuremberg (2025 film)"), starring Rami Malek, Russell Crowe and Michael Shannon as Robert H. Jackson.[[113]](#cite_note-114)

## Names for *Kristallnacht*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kristallnacht&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Names for Kristallnacht")]

The events of November 1938 have been given disparate names by the perpetrators, bystanders, victims, and historians. In the post-war years, the terms *Kristallnacht* and *Reichskristallnacht* became established in West Germany and abroad. In [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany"), the state typically referred to the events as "fascist pogrom night" (German: *"faschistische Pogromnacht"*).[[114]](#cite_note-ja-115) Despite speculation that the term was coined by the Nazis,[[115]](#cite_note-rbb-116) there is no documented written use of the term before the end of World War II.[[3]](#cite_note-toi-3) Despite this, many people later recalled the term being used at the time, leading historians to conclude the term originated from the general public.[[116]](#cite_note-ashkenaz-117)[[3]](#cite_note-toi-3)[[117]](#cite_note-dlf-118)

Beginning in the late 1970s, alternative names for *Kristallnacht* were introduced as the "postwar generations began challenging their parents' sanitized version of history",[[3]](#cite_note-toi-3) and by the 1980s, the "harmless-sounding" term "*Kristallnacht*" began to be supplanted in the German-speaking world by several alternatives, such as *Reichspogromnacht*, *Novemberpogrome*, and *Novemberterror*.[[3]](#cite_note-toi-3)[[118]](#cite_note-baumann-119) Linguistically and historically, the term *Kristallnacht* is seen as problematic. It is widely seen as a euphemism that trivializes anti-Jewish violence,[[115]](#cite_note-rbb-116)[[119]](#cite_note-120)[[120]](#cite_note-barkai-121)[[121]](#cite_note-taz-122) which avoids mentioning either the perpetrators or that the murders, looting, and arson were officially encouraged by the state.[[122]](#cite_note-jmb-123)[[118]](#cite_note-baumann-119): 8 Focusing on "broken glass" places vandalism in the foreground and ignores the murders that were committed:[[3]](#cite_note-toi-3)[[122]](#cite_note-jmb-123) [Heinz Galinski](/wiki/Heinz_Galinski "Heinz Galinski"), the former president of the [Central Council of Jews in Germany](/wiki/Central_Council_of_Jews_in_Germany "Central Council of Jews in Germany"), criticized the term in 1978, stating "more than just glass was broken. People were killed."[[3]](#cite_note-toi-3) The use of the word "night" belies the fact that much of the violence was committed in broad daylight, and the violence was not confined to a single night.[[122]](#cite_note-jmb-123)[[123]](#cite_note-blpb-124) Many of the [30,000 Jews arrested following *Kristallnacht*](/wiki/Mass_arrests_after_Kristallnacht "Mass arrests after Kristallnacht") were still imprisoned in concentration camps months later. The term's unclear origins also suggest it originated from the German public and not the event's victims.[[124]](#cite_note-Freitag-125) Although no evidence has been found to confirm this, "*Kristallnacht*" is still perceived by many as a Nazi coinage.[[115]](#cite_note-rbb-116)

Although *Novemberpogrome* is today the dominant term, some historians and authors have also criticized the use of the term "[pogrom](/wiki/Pogrom "Pogrom")", as it incorporates associations that can be interpreted as [trivializing](/wiki/Holocaust_trivialization "Holocaust trivialization") the events.[[114]](#cite_note-ja-115)[[118]](#cite_note-baumann-119): 13 The scale of *Kristallnacht* far outstripped any [pogroms in the Russian Empire](/wiki/Pogroms_in_the_Russian_Empire "Pogroms in the Russian Empire"), which were local or regional events of spontaneous, lawless mass-violence against Jews and their property. These massacres were initiated by a local population with at most the tacit approval of the local authorities and police. In contrast, the events of *Kristallnacht* were centrally organized by the national government and systematically carried out across the country, a connotation which is missing from the word "pogrom".[[118]](#cite_note-baumann-119)[[125]](#cite_note-126)[[122]](#cite_note-jmb-123) The word "pogrom" also places the events in a context that suggests the [Holocaust was not unique](/wiki/Holocaust_uniqueness_debate "Holocaust uniqueness debate").[[121]](#cite_note-taz-122) Furthermore, the word "*Kristallnacht*" is established in other languages, and divergent vocabulary can add confusion and difficulty when communicating with researchers and people abroad.[[3]](#cite_note-toi-3)

On the 80th anniversary, the president of the [German Historical Museum](/wiki/German_Historical_Museum "German Historical Museum") stated that there "will never be" an adequate name for the events of *Kristallnacht*.[[126]](#cite_note-dlf2-127)
